Houthi rebels in Yemen on Monday announced they were imposing a maritime embargo against Saudi Arabia in yet another threat to global petroleum supplies amid the US war on Iran. Yahya Saree, spokesperson for the Houthis, said in a video announcement that the militia would be enforcing the shipping embargo in retaliation for last week’s bombing of Sanaa Airport, which the […] The post Houthis announce maritime embargo of Saudi Arabia appeared first on Asia Times.

corpus.title: Yemen's Houthis announce 'maritime embargo' on Saudi Arabia
corpus.signal_text:
Yemen's Houthis announce 'maritime embargo' on Saudi Arabia
- Published
Yemen's Houthis have announced a "maritime embargo" against Saudi Arabia.
The Iran-backed group's military spokesman said it would begin immediately and was in retaliation for a Saudi blockade of ports and airports in Houthi-controlled north-western Yemen.
He gave few details about the embargo, but a Houthi media official said they were closing the Bab al-Mandab Strait - the Red Sea's southern gateway - to Saudi vessels.
In response, Saudi Arabia's foreign ministry condemned the allegation, vowing that the country "will take all necessary measures to protect its ships in accordance with international law".
The Red Sea has become crucial for oil shipments from Saudi Arabia during the US-Israeli war with Iran, which has led to the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz in the Gulf.
It comes a week after the most significant escalation in the largely dormant conflict between the Houthis and Saudi Arabia since an informal truce took effect in 2022.
The Houthis said they had launched missiles at Abha airport in south-western Saudi Arabia in response to air strikes on Sanaa's airport that they blamed on the kingdom.
The strikes were claimed by the Saudi-backed Yemeni government, which said it had wanted to prevent an Iranian plane from landing in the capital without authorisation.
Yemen has been devastated by a civil war that began in 2014, when the Houthis ousted the government from Sanaa. The conflict escalated in 2015, after the Saudi-led coalition of Arab states intervened in an attempt to restore the government's rule.
The fighting has reportedly left more than 150,000 people dead and triggered one of the world's worst humanitarian crises, with more than 22 million people in need of some form of aid, according to the UN.
On Monday, Houthi military spokesman Yahya Sarea said Saudi Arabia had imposed an "unjust and oppressive siege on our dear people for nearly 12 years, plundering our resources and imposing a comprehensive blockade on our ports and airports by land, sea, and air".
He then announced that the group had decided to respond by declaring a "maritime embargo against the criminal Saudi enemy, based on the equation of 'an eye for an eye'".
"Any foolish act committed by the reckless Saudi enemy through all escalation will be met with a comprehensive and decisive escalation," he added.
Sarea did not provide any further information about the embargo, but the deputy head of the Ho

corpus.title: Houthis announce Saudi naval blockade, threatening new front in US-Iran war
corpus.signal_text:
Houthis announce Saudi naval blockade, threatening new front in US-Iran war

DUBAI/RIYADH, July 20 (Reuters) - Yemen’s Iran-aligned Houthis said on Monday they would impose a naval blockade on Saudi Arabia, opening a potential new front against the United States in its war with Iran and raising the threat to global energy supplies and trade beyond the Gulf.

corpus.title: Saudi Arabia fumes after Yemen's Houthis impose naval blockade
corpus.signal_text:
© Turkuvaz Haberleşme ve Yayıncılık 2026
                        
                    
            				
									by Agence France-Presse - AFP
								
            			Jul 20, 2026 11:29 pm
        
        Saudi Arabia on Monday condemned an announcement by Yemen's Houthis of a maritime blockade of the kingdom, as the Iran-backed rebels threatened the top oil exporter's ability to bypass the Strait of Hormuz.
In a statement, the Gulf monarchy's Foreign Ministry said it "expresses the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia's strongest condemnation of the statements made by the so-called military spokesman of the terrorist Houthi militia ... imposing a maritime blockade on the Kingdom."
It added that Riyadh "continues to support the brotherly Yemeni people and their legitimate government."
Earlier, the Houthis announced a maritime embargo of Saudi Arabia, stepping up the U.S.-Iran war.
The group did not say how it planned to impose the embargo, whose declaration came as Iran said it was back to a "full-scale war" with the United States and as Washington and Tehran battled to control the strait.
Saudi Arabia and the Houthis traded fire last week for the first time in years, threatening a 2022 truce that has held despite expiring.
The exchange of strikes was set off when an Iranian plane attempted to land in Sanaa, challenging Saudi Arabia's hegemony over Yemeni skies.
The Houthis' naval blockade, if enforced, could further strain Saudi Arabia's economy by cutting off access to its Red Sea ports, which have been vital for oil exports as renewed fighting limits passage through the Strait of Hormuz.
The blockade is also expected to hurt global markets that rely on Saudi Arabia, which produces over 10 million barrels of crude per day, for energy imports.
The rebels declared "a maritime embargo against the criminal Saudi enemy, based on the equation of 'an eye for an eye,' effective immediately," military spokesperson Yahya Saree said.
Stephane Dujarric, spokesman for the U.N. secretary-general, warned that the threat from the Houthis risked an "even wider" regional conflict.
                    The Daily Sabah Newsletter

corpus.title: Houthis’ Bab-el-Mandeb Blockade Could Disrupt Crude Supply to S. Korea
corpus.signal_text:
[4K] YEONJUN, AHOF, RESCENE, EVAN, KIHYUN, CHOI YOOJUNG, VAYONN | On the way to Music Bank 260710
2026-07-10
Written: 2026-07-21 17:41:15 / Updated: 2026-07-21 18:28:51
Photo : EPA via YONHAP News
Kim Bum-soo has more.
Report: Yemen’s Houthi rebels say they are imposing a maritime embargo against Saudi Arabia to retaliate for a recent attack on Sanaa International Airport.
[Sound bite: Houthi military spokesman Brig. Gen. Yahya Saree (Arabic-English)]
“The Yemeni armed forces declare the ban on maritime navigation on the criminal Saudi enemy according to an equation of a siege by siege. The implementation phase will enter from the moment of the announcement of this statement …”
A military spokesperson for the Iran-backed Houthis announced the decision in a video statement Monday, threatening to block shipping through the Bab-el-Mandeb Strait.
Some seven-point-four million barrels of crude oil, or some seven percent of global oil production, passes though the waterway’s narrowest point on a daily basis.
Since the Strait of Hormuz was closed, South Korea has relied on the Bab-el-Mandeb gateway as an alternative route to purchase Saudi crude transported to the Red Sea port of Yanbu.
More than 80 percent of the Saudi crude transported to Yanbu is purchased by South Korea, Japan and other East Asian energy importers.
If the Red Sea gateway is closed, delivering Middle Eastern crude to East Asia will mean taking an inefficient detour through the Suez Canal into the Mediterranean Sea and virtually circling the African continent.
According to the South Korean oceans ministry on Tuesday, a total of 15 South Korean tankers have passed through the Red Sea to transport Saudi crude oil to the country since the Strait of Hormuz was blocked in late February.
There are currently no South Korean vessels anchored in the Red Sea, according to the government.
The South Korean government says the country’s crude supply is sufficient for the time being.
It has secured more than 90 percent of its required crude oil supply for September.
Kim Bum-soo, KBS World Radio News.

corpus.title: Houthis declare naval blockade of Saudi Arabia
corpus.signal_text:
Houthis declare naval blockade of Saudi Arabia
Yemen’s Iran-backed Houthi rebels have declared a naval blockade of Saudi Arabia, marking the biggest escalation between the two sides in years.
The move came a week after forces loyal to Yemen’s Saudi-backed government bombed Sana’a International Airport in an attempt to prevent an Iranian plane carrying a Houthi delegation returning from Tehran from landing.
In a statement on Monday, Houthi military spokesman Yahya Saree condemned what he called an “unjust and oppressive siege” of the country.
“The Yemeni Armed Forces declare a maritime embargo against the criminal Saudi enemy, based on the equation of ‘an eye for an eye,’ effective immediately,” Saree said in a video posted on X.
The spokesman warned that “any foolish act” by Saudi Arabia “will be met with a comprehensive and decisive escalation.”
The Saudi Foreign Ministry condemned “the Houthi terrorist militia” and vowed to “take all necessary measures to protect its ships in accordance with international law.”
The Houthis, a Shiite movement, seized Yemen’s capital, Sana’a, during the country’s civil war in 2014. A year later, Saudi Arabia led a military intervention on behalf of the internationally recognized government.
While the Saudi-led bombing campaign was unable to dislodge the Houthis, it was condemned by the UN and human rights organizations, worsened Yemen’s humanitarian crisis and displaced millions of people.
The Houthis responded to the bombing by launching missiles and drones at targets in Saudi Arabia, including airports and energy facilities.
After the war in Gaza broke out in 2023, the group began attacking ships in the Red Sea in what it described as an act of solidarity with the Palestinians. Those attacks largely ceased following a US-Houthi ceasefire in May 2025.
The security of the Red Sea, which is linked to the Mediterranean Sea through the Suez Canal and to the Indian Ocean via the Bab el-Mandeb Strait, is vital to global shipping.
The announcement of the blockade comes as the Strait of Hormuz, another key maritime chokepoint, remains closed due to the ongoing US-Iran war. The US launched strikes against Iran for a tenth consecutive night on Tuesday, with both sides signaling that the truce was effectively over.
